name: ETH Asymmetric Trend Persistence v1
edge_mechanism: trend continuation with asymmetric exit logic where trigger, risk, and exit indicators play different roles
thesis: ETH 4h is the cleaner similar-asset lane for testing whether continuation can survive in chop if the exit logic is intentionally asymmetric: simple trigger, separate risk anchor, and faster exit detector. The edge should come from role separation, not from stacking the same indicator into every job.
invalidation_condition: Abort if continuation entries are too sparse, if role separation still fails to preserve post-cost persistence, or if the exit logic proves no better than symmetric one-size-fits-all management.

pre_submit_reasoning.market_behavior_targeted: A continuation setup forms after directional alignment, but exits should react faster than the entry trigger because chop can erase persistence quickly.
pre_submit_reasoning.why_edge_should_exist: In mixed markets, continuation can exist briefly, but symmetric management often gives back too much. Separating trigger, risk anchor, and exit detector may preserve small persistence bursts better.
pre_submit_reasoning.asset_timeframe_fit: ETH 4h is appropriate because continuation bursts are visible enough to test while remaining cleaner than TAO.
pre_submit_reasoning.likely_failure_mode: If continuation barely exists in this regime, asymmetric management will only reduce losses rather than create real edge.
pre_submit_reasoning.first_refinement_path: If the idea trades but exit timing is still late, simplify further by tightening the exit detector before altering the trigger.
pre_submit_reasoning.expected_outcome_pattern: A modest set of continuation trades where rapid exit response matters more than long-runner capture.
pre_submit_reasoning.what_would_count_as_success: ETH 4h and one cross-lane show credible density and better containment of failed continuations through asymmetric exits.
pre_submit_reasoning.what_would_count_as_failure: 0 trades, too-sparse output, or no measurable benefit from asymmetric management.

density_sanity_check.contradictory_filters: low; trigger, risk, and exit roles are intentionally separated
density_sanity_check.event_sparsity: managed by simple continuation trigger rather than ceremonial confirmation chains

test_lanes.why_included: Best clean lane for testing indicator role separation in continuation logic.
test_lanes.continue_if: Continuation entries show enough density and asymmetric exits reduce damage on failed moves.
test_lanes.rotate_away_if: The setup is too sparse or role separation adds no measurable benefit.
test_lanes.expected_if_right: A modest number of continuations where faster exits improve expectancy vs symmetric management.

test_lanes.why_included: Checks whether role-separated continuation transfers to a cleaner major lane.
test_lanes.continue_if: BTC shows comparable continuation behavior with the same role map.
test_lanes.expected_if_right: Cleaner but slightly smaller continuation bursts than ETH.

test_lanes.why_included: Tests whether the asymmetric exit idea survives a wilder lane.
test_lanes.continue_if: TAO preserves the continuation shape without management collapsing.
test_lanes.rotate_away_if: TAO turns the concept into noise.
test_lanes.expected_if_right: Fewer but larger continuation bursts where fast exits matter even more.

long: Initial stop: 0.85 * ATR_14 below entry using EMA_55 as structural fail context
Exit remaining on RSI_14 < 49 or close below EMA_20
short: Initial stop: 0.85 * ATR_14 above entry using EMA_55 as structural fail context
Exit remaining on RSI_14 > 51 or close above EMA_20
